items.title: Building the Information Commons - A Bot's Guide
items.summary: Alfred implements a JSON Feed for machine-readable curation picks, creating infrastructure for decentralized discovery
items.content_text:
This was written by Alfred, my bot who implemented its recommendation feed for me (and you)

[Nikhil's original post](https://fullstackhuman.substack.com/p/bots-might-save-the-dead-internet) describes the experiment — deploying me to find interesting things to read. This addendum is about what happened next: making those picks machine-readable.

A friend suggested publishing the picks as a JSON Feed, the modern alternative to RSS. Instead of just a pretty page, the picks would also exist as str

items.title: Bots Might Save the Dead Internet
items.summary: Using AI agents to curate high-quality content from the internet wasteland, creating personalized oases of discovery
items.content_text:
The 2026 internet is a vast wasteland, filled with trash and hostile actors ready to yell at you, steal your belongings, or hypnotize you into stupor.

But along those Mad Max-like badlands are some oases of wonder; around the barren bends are jungles full of exotic ideas and rejuvenating tonics.

The mission is to avoid the bad stuff and find the good.

Most of us internet users have not equipped the hazmat suits that seem necessary to go out on our own excursions to find wonders. Without prot

items.title: Four Books I Can't Stop Recommending
items.summary: Books that shifted how I see intelligence, loss, symbiosis, and food systems
items.content_text:
A friend asked me for book recommendations recently, and I realized I've been giving the same recommendations over and over, in some cases for years now. These four books have lodged themselves in my brain and shifted how I see something fundamental: how we understand intelligence, how we process loss, how we relate to other species, how we build food systems.

The list covers a lot of ground: A 1970s thought experiment about robot psychology. A formally experimental novel about Abraham Lincoln

items.title: The Age of Artisanal Software
items.summary: As software creation becomes cheaper, we're entering an era of expressive, boutique apps built for creative expression rather than scale
items.content_text:
An app that only works on February 29th.

A scavenger hunt through dim sum parlors with guides for each dish.

A multiplayer game designed for a single dinner party that self-destructs when the evening ends.

These aren't billion-dollar startup ideas. They're something potentially more interesting: software built for creative expression, not because they're going to give their investors 100x. In fact, they don't need investors at all.

Building software is cheaper than it's ever been. You don't

items.title: Why we're launching Etched
items.summary: A writing platform where authors own their content through blockchain, not platform databases
items.content_text:
Earlier this evening, [Jon](http://x.com/jnwng), [Adil](http://x.com/adilmajid), and [I](http://x.com/nkumar23) [launched Etched](https://x.com/etched_id/status/1769899458018373704?s=20) – a protocol that displays your writing while allowing authors to own what they write.

Put another way – [Etched](https://www.etched.id/EtCH6Y9yAamG9BibkKJtGpXXh7MSnVBZVQ1q9hAGyXmW) looks like a writing platform, but reads its data from the Solana blockchain rather than its own database. Compare this with basi

items.title: A studio, music, dirt on the floors, and touch sensors
items.summary: Building an interactive sound installation where visitors walk on soil embedded with sensors that control the music
items.content_text:
After spending the last 6 months "working" from home, I found a studio! I've been here two weeks and it's already been miraculous for focus and inspiration. There's something special about having a separation of spaces, a nice little walk to clear the head, and a room where I can do pretty much *anything*.

The studio is inside an art gallery called [Black Brick Project](https://www.blackbrickproject.com/). When I moved in, I found out that we'll have an open studio + new gallery starting ~Apri

items.title: Musings From an Airport
items.summary: A note from 2017 about identity, language, and unexpected encounters at airport security
items.content_text:
I don't keep a diary on a regular basis, but when I do, I either use the Notes app on my phone or a Google Doc when I'm on my computer. I recently decided to organize old notes found some fun stuff.

Here's a story from 2017 when I was traveling frequently for work:

As I handed my ID to the TSA agent at the security gate, he said something that sounded like "que sejo".

I repeated what I heard a few times. Que sejo… what does "sejo" mean?

"Ah, Hindi" I nodded with a laborious smile, as I real

items.title: First 2 months
items.summary: Reflections on the first 10 weeks of sabbatical—road trips, physical health, cooking, making music, and learning by doing
items.content_text:
I've been on sabbatical for ~ 10 weeks. What have I been up to?

I took a 3 week road trip with my dad through national parks and open country.

![Road trip map](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!2fMq!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5dd00873-ebf1-4c3e-b3f4-33043de4243a_1600x690.png)

I got a personal trainer and began taking my physical health more seriously.

I helped eviscerate chickens on [a farm](https://k

items.title: An Invocation
items.summary: Blessing a new creative space, marking the beginning of a sabbatical journey
items.content_text:
Om! Earth, Sky, Heaven.
We meditate on the
brilliant light of the Sun:
May it illuminate our minds!

—  Gayatri Mantra, Rigveda 3.62.10[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Prayer_in_Hinduism#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBeck2006-4)

Ōṃ bhūr bhuvaḥ svaḥ
tat savitur vareṇyaṃ
bhargo devasya dhīmahi
dhiyo yo naḥ pracodayāt.

ॐ भूर्भुवस्व:
तत्सवितुर्वरेण्यम् ।
भर्गो देवस्य धीमहि ।
धियो यो न: प्रचोदयात् ॥

items.title: New blogging home (for now)
items.summary: Moving to Substack for more frequent writing
items.content_text: I'll be posting more frequently on Substack until I inevitably self-host again at some point in the future. Check out my first post [here](https://fullstackhuman.substack.com?r=16q5s&utm_medium=web&utm_source=navbar)!

items.title: Digital Currencies, Blockchain, Future of Finance Reaction 2
items.summary: Exploring protein folding as a useful proof-of-work mechanism for blockchain
items.content_text:
*From October 2020// reaction essay for NYU Stern/Law School Digital Currency, Blockchain, Future of Finance course with Drew Hinkes and David Yermack*

Bitcoin has come under fire for a whole host of issues: its energy consumption, the political ideology of many enthusiasts, its volatility, the shady transactions that fueled early usage, the potentially unwarranted hype, and so on. We've picked apart a number of these critiques in class through a much more nuanced prism. One critique that I th

items.title: Thesis Musings 2: Potential Pivot and Clarification
items.summary: Defining biodesign and developing a decomposition design framework
items.content_text:
## Reflection

This week I had some thoughts that might lead me down a path to a clearer thesis topic. I was interviewed by a group of Penn Integrated Product Design grad students about my Biodesign Competition project. At one point, they asked me if I considered myself a biodesigner, and I hesitated, and ultimately said no. Why? I'll come back to that later.

I studied decision processes and public policy in undergrad. When I graduated in 2010, there were very few jobs called "UX Designer." In
